title: Dissipative Dynamics and Active Stabilization of Linear and Nonlinear Waves in Non-PT-Symmetric Harmonic Traps
abstract: We investigate the dissipative dynamics of linear and nonlinear waves in harmonic traps by means of engineered complex non-Hermitian potentials. By combining an analytical mapping between real and complex Schrödinger equations with direct numerical simulations, we show that while in the linear case the damped motion leads to the formation of a stationary state at the trap center, in the nonlinear case a static potential design alone is insufficient to ensure long-term stability. Instead, the system relaxes toward a long-lived metastable configuration that eventually undergoes decay or collapse. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a time-dependent modulation of the nonlinearity that effectively converts these metastable states into robust non-equilibrium stationary states. This approach establishes a general strategy for controlling nonlinear waves in non-Hermitian systems, with potential applications in photonics and Bose--Einstein condensates.
summary: The paper demonstrates that while static complex potentials can stabilize linear waves, they lead to the decay or collapse of nonlinear waves due to gain-loss imbalances. By implementing a time-periodic modulation of the nonlinearity, the authors show it is possible to actively stabilize these waves into robust non-equilibrium stationary states.
main_problem: Investigating the instability of nonlinear waves in non-PT-symmetric harmonic traps, where static complex potentials fail to prevent the decay or collapse of nonlinear wave packets.
main_result: The introduction of a time-dependent modulation of the nonlinearity (nonlinear management) can effectively stabilize metastable nonlinear states into robust non-equilibrium stationary states (NESS).
method: The study uses an analytical mapping between real and complex Nonlinear Schrödinger equations combined with direct numerical simulations of wave packet dynamics.
model_or_system: A normalized nonlinear Schrödinger (NLS) equation with a complex non-Hermitian potential $V(x) = V_R(x) + iW(x)$ representing gain and loss, applicable to photonics and Bose-Einstein condensates.
key_observables: Wavefunction amplitude, density $|\psi(x, t)|^2$, center-of-mass position $x_c(t)$, and peak density amplitude.
important_parameters: Nonlinearity strength $\sigma$ (attractive, repulsive, or linear), modulation frequency $\Omega$, and modulation amplitude $\gamma$.
main_assumptions: The stabilization relies on the assumption that active time-dependent modulation can compensate for the residual gain-loss imbalance inherent in nonlinear regimes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the engineered potential and damped linear dynamics; Figure 2 compares the onset of instability (collapse/decay) in linear vs. nonlinear cases; Figure 3 and 5 illustrate the spatio-temporal evolution and the formation of robust NESS under the nonlinear management protocol.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the complex potential engineering via analytical mapping, moves to demonstrating stable damped motion in the linear regime, explores the resulting instability in the nonlinear regime, and concludes by presenting a stabilization strategy via periodic nonlinearity modulation.
why_interesting: It provides a general strategy for controlling non-equilibrium dynamics in open quantum systems, specifically addressing how to prevent the collapse of nonlinear waves in dissipative environments like BECs or lossy photonic waveguides.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 3. Spatio-temporal evolution of the wave amplitude |ψ(x, t)| in the nonlinear regime for σ = −1 (left panel) and σ = 1 (right panel). The figures show the complete dissipative relaxation toward the trap center during the early stage of the dynamics, followed by the formation of long-lived metastable states. On longer timescales, the attractive case (σ = −1) un- dergoes collapse, whereas the repulsive case (σ = 1) exhibits a slow decay of the wave amplitude. The complex potential and parameter values are the same as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 2. Time evolution of the center of mass (left panel) and peak amplitude (right panel) for the same complex potential used in the linear case, illustrating the effect of nonlinearity on the dissipative dynamics. The blue and red solid lines correspond to attractive (σ = −1) and repulsive (σ = 1) interactions, respectively, while the dashed black line (σ = 0) is shown for comparison. The logarithmic scale highlights the slow evolution of the metastable states. Parameters are the same as in Fig. 1, except for x0 = 2.0, a1 = −0.1, and a2 = −0.1.

FIG. 4. Time evolution of the peak amplitude for attractive, σ0 = −1 (left panel), and repulsive, σ0 = 1 (right panel) in the presence of NM nonlinear (continuous curves) with differ- ent values of γ. The continuus curves, with magenta, orange, brown, green, colors correspond in both panels to the am- plitudes γ = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0., respectively. The dashed blue, black, and red lines refer to the value γ = 0 (i.e. absence of NM) and coincide with the curves of the same color de- picted in Fig. 3 here plotted is linear scale. The modulation frequency is Ω= 0.2 and the activation times are t0 = 100 and t0 = 80 for left and right panels, respectively. All other parameters are the same as in...

FIG. 5. Spatio-temporal evolution of the wave amplitude |ψ(x, t)| in the presence of the NM protocol in Eq. (26) with γ = −2 and σ0 = −1, t0 = 100 (left panel), σ = 1, t0 = 75 (right panel). All other parameters are fixed as in Fig. 3.
